name: Topical rapamycin reduced human skin p16 senescence marker
summary: Topical rapamycin provides a direct but local human biomarker signal for rapamycin-class biology; it should support Rapalogs only as boundary-limited skin evidence, not systemic rejuvenation.
statement: In an exploratory placebo-controlled human skin trial, topical rapamycin was associated with reduced p16INK4A protein expression, increased collagen VII, and clinical or histologic skin-aging improvements in multiple participants.
population_or_model: Adults over 40 with age-related photoaging and dermal volume loss
time_horizon: End-of-study topical treatment follow-up in a small dermatology trial
quantitative_note: The PubMed abstract reports 36 participants enrolled, 19 discontinued or were lost to follow-up, p16INK4A reduction with P = 0.008, and collagen VII increase with P = 0.0077.
caveats: The trial was exploratory and local to skin, with substantial discontinuation or loss to follow-up.
p16 reduction is a senescence biomarker signal, not proof of durable systemic rejuvenation.
Topical rapamycin does not establish the benefit-risk profile of systemic rapalog use for broad aging prevention.
